hypothesis: In hospital EHR data, a time-aware sequence model trained on the last 24 hours of vitals, labs, medications, and nursing events will predict 6-hour in-hospital sepsis onset more accurately than a static feature baseline built from aggregated summary statistics, when both are evaluated on future admissions from unseen months.
domain: Healthcare / clinical ML — inpatient sepsis early warning using electronic health records
available_data: A large hospital system would realistically have de-identified EHR data for ICU and non-ICU admissions spanning multiple years, including timestamped vitals, lab results, medication administrations, orders, and diagnosis/procedure codes. Sepsis labels can be derived retrospectively from clinical criteria and charted onset times, with tens of thousands of admissions and relatively sparse positive cases across the time window.
success_metric_context: The stakeholder cares about identifying patients likely to deteriorate early enough for clinicians to intervene, so higher recall at clinically acceptable false-alert rates is more important than raw accuracy. Operationally, the goal is to reduce missed sepsis cases while keeping alert burden manageable for bedside staff.
design_challenges: The data are temporally ordered and label leakage is a risk because some labs, treatments, or charted codes may occur after the true onset time, so the evaluation must respect chronology and prediction horizons.
Sepsis incidence is highly imbalanced and onset time is noisy/partially observed, making the choice of labeling window and calibration threshold critical to a fair comparison.
known_confounds: Any observed improvement could be due to better handling of missingness patterns or more recent care-process changes rather than true temporal modeling power, so a critic would check whether both models are matched on the same feature information and tested on held-out future months.

hypothesis: A transformer-based model fine-tuned on historical radiology reports and associated follow-up pathology labels will predict clinically significant incidental findings on chest CT better than a bag-of-words TF-IDF baseline, as measured by AUROC and calibration on a temporally held-out cohort.
domain: Healthcare / clinical ML for radiology report-based risk prediction in a hospital imaging workflow
available_data: A large hospital system would realistically have de-identified chest CT radiology reports, structured report metadata, and downstream labels from pathology, follow-up imaging, or clinician adjudication over several years. The dataset could span hundreds of thousands of studies, with labels available only for a subset of scans where follow-up confirms whether an incidental finding was clinically significant.
success_metric_context: Radiology teams care about correctly flagging scans that need expedited review or follow-up without overwhelming clinicians with false alarms. Better performance means higher sensitivity at a fixed alert budget and well-calibrated probabilities so that triage thresholds can be set safely.
design_challenges: The data are temporally ordered and clinical practice changes over time, so evaluation should avoid random splits that leak reporting language or downstream care patterns into test data.

Positive labels are sparse and selectively verified because only some incidental findings receive follow-up confirmation, creating label bias and class imbalance that can distort apparent model gains.
known_confounds: Any improvement could be driven by the transformer learning report template artifacts, scanner/site differences, or downstream documentation patterns rather than true clinical understanding of incidental findings.

hypothesis: A transformer-based named entity recognition model fine-tuned on full clinical notes will identify medication, dosage, and adverse-event mentions more accurately than a CRF model using hand-engineered lexical and section features for post-discharge pharmacovigilance extraction.
domain: Healthcare / clinical NLP for adverse event extraction from EHR discharge summaries and follow-up notes
available_data: Hospitals commonly store large archives of de-identified discharge summaries, progress notes, and follow-up messages with medication lists and documented adverse drug events. A realistic dataset would span several years of notes from one or more health systems, with a smaller subset manually annotated by clinicians or pharmacists for entity spans and relation labels; note length, department, and medication class are all naturally available metadata.
success_metric_context: Clinicians and safety teams care about finding medication side effects and dosing errors early enough to support chart review and intervention. Better performance means higher span-level F1 on clinically important entities, especially adverse-event mentions with low prevalence and high cost of misses.
design_challenges: Clinical notes have strong temporal and section structure, so the evaluation must avoid leakage across notes from the same patient and likely requires patient-level or encounter-level splits.

Annotations are highly imbalanced and ambiguous because the same phrase can be a confirmed adverse event, a ruled-out concern, or a family history mention; the experiment must define labels and scope carefully.
known_confounds: Any apparent advantage of the transformer could be due to access to richer context or pretrained clinical vocabulary rather than sequence modeling itself, so a critic would check whether gains persist when both models use the same input fields and comparable preprocessing.

hypothesis: A longitudinal transformer trained on raw EHR event sequences will predict 30-day hospital readmission more accurately than a gradient-boosted model trained on handcrafted summary features, especially for patients with irregular care histories.
domain: Healthcare / clinical ML in inpatient and post-discharge risk prediction using electronic health records
available_data: Hospital EHR data would realistically include demographics, diagnoses, procedures, medications, lab results, vitals, encounter histories, and discharge outcomes for tens of thousands of admissions over multiple years. Labels for 30-day readmission are usually available from claims or hospital encounter logs, while feature timestamps allow construction of patient trajectories before discharge. The data are naturally longitudinal and often span multiple departments and care settings.
success_metric_context: Clinically, the goal is to identify patients at high risk of returning soon after discharge so that follow-up resources, care coordination, or medication reconciliation can be targeted efficiently. Better performance means higher discrimination and better calibration at clinically relevant alert thresholds, not just higher overall accuracy.
design_challenges: The temporal structure is non-trivial: information leakage can occur if labs, codes, or notes recorded after discharge are inadvertently included, so the split and feature cutoff must be strictly time-aware.

Readmission is strongly affected by confounding care-process factors such as discharge disposition, length of stay, and prior utilization, so the evaluation must account for class imbalance and likely distribution shift across wards or years.
known_confounds: Any observed improvement could be driven by the transformer exploiting sequence length or documentation density rather than truly learning richer clinical trajectories, so a careful critic would compare calibration, subgroup performance, and sensitivity to reduced-history inputs.

hypothesis: A transformer-based sequence model trained on longitudinal ICU electronic health record data will predict 72-hour in-hospital mortality better than a gradient-boosted model on hand-engineered summary features, when both are evaluated on the same prospective hospital holdout cohort.
domain: Healthcare / clinical ML — intensive care unit risk prediction from EHR data
available_data: This hypothesis could be tested on de-identified ICU EHR data containing time-stamped vitals, lab results, medication administrations, procedures, and demographics across multiple hospital admissions over several years. Labels such as in-hospital mortality within a fixed 72-hour horizon are routinely available from discharge outcomes, and the dataset would likely include tens of thousands of patient stays with irregularly sampled measurements.
success_metric_context: Clinically, better means earlier and more reliable identification of patients at high risk of deterioration so clinicians can intervene sooner. Operationally, the model should improve discrimination and calibration at the decision threshold used for alerts, while keeping false alarms low enough to be actionable.
design_challenges: The data are temporally irregular and partially observed, so the experiment must decide how to represent missingness and avoid leakage from measurements taken after the prediction point.

Mortality is relatively rare in ICU cohorts, so class imbalance and calibration across subpopulations matter; a model with higher AUROC but poor positive predictive value may not be useful in practice.
known_confounds: Any apparent gain could be driven by the sequence model capturing documentation intensity or care escalation patterns rather than true physiologic signal, so a careful critic would check whether performance persists under matched-feature and timestamp-ablation controls.

hypothesis: For municipal traffic demand forecasting, a temporal graph neural network that incorporates road-network adjacency and weather covariates will produce lower 30-minute-ahead prediction error than a per-sensor gradient-boosted regression baseline trained only on lagged traffic counts and calendar features.
domain: Transportation / logistics — citywide traffic flow forecasting from connected road sensors and weather feeds
available_data: A city transportation department or highway authority would typically have minute-level or 5-minute traffic counts/speeds from loop detectors, radar sensors, or connected vehicle probes across dozens to hundreds of road segments, along with timestamps, incident logs, and weather observations over multiple years. The label is the future traffic volume or speed at each sensor location, observed continuously and suitable for supervised forecasting.
success_metric_context: The stakeholder cares about reducing forecast error enough to improve signal timing, congestion management, and incident response. Better means more accurate short-horizon predictions during rush hour, storms, and special events, since those are the times operational decisions matter most.
design_challenges: The data are strongly autocorrelated and non-stationary, so the evaluation must use chronological splits that prevent leakage across adjacent time periods and seasonal cycles.

Rare disruptive events such as crashes, roadworks, and severe weather can dominate practical value but are sparsely labeled, making it difficult to distinguish genuine modeling gains from better handling of outliers or missing data.
known_confounds: Any improvement could be driven by the graph model’s ability to exploit spatial smoothing or by better robustness to missing sensor readings rather than the explicit use of network structure and weather covariates.

hypothesis: A transformer-based anomaly detector trained on raw machine telemetry sequences will identify incipient bearing failures earlier and with higher event-level recall than a feature-engineered gradient-boosted baseline for predictive maintenance in industrial equipment.
domain: Manufacturing / industrial predictive maintenance for rotating machinery in factory sensor data
available_data: Factories and OEMs commonly collect high-frequency vibration, temperature, pressure, current, and RPM telemetry from machines such as pumps, motors, and compressors, along with maintenance logs and failure timestamps. A realistic dataset would span months to years across many assets, with relatively few labeled failure events and many unlabeled normal operating periods; labels may come from work orders, part replacements, or failure reports. The data are typically time-stamped, multi-sensor, and irregularly sampled, with missingness and sensor drift.
success_metric_context: Maintenance teams care about detecting failures early enough to schedule service before catastrophic downtime, not just flagging anomalies after the fact. Better performance means higher recall on true failure events at a fixed false-alarm rate, and more lead time before actual breakdowns so technicians can intervene.
design_challenges: The data are temporally dependent, so the evaluation must use a chronological split to avoid leakage from future operating regimes and maintenance outcomes.

Failures are rare and the cost of false positives is asymmetric, so choosing an event-level metric and thresholding scheme is non-trivial; window-based labeling around failure times can substantially affect results.
known_confounds: Any apparent advantage could be due to the transformer exploiting differences in sensor coverage, sampling frequency, or maintenance documentation quality rather than a superior representation of degradation dynamics.

hypothesis: A gradient-boosted model trained on raw clickstream, course-activity, and assessment-sequence features will predict end-of-term student dropout more accurately than a baseline logistic regression on aggregated engagement counts for an online education platform, when evaluated on future course cohorts.
domain: Education / online learning platform student outcome prediction
available_data: The platform would realistically have per-student event logs from video views, quiz attempts, forum activity, assignment submissions, and timestamps across multiple course runs, along with end-of-term labels such as dropout, completion, or withdrawal. A few academic terms or years of historical cohorts would provide tens of thousands of students with sequence-level behavior data and known outcomes, with labels available after the course ends.
success_metric_context: The stakeholder cares about identifying at-risk students early enough to trigger interventions like tutoring, reminders, or outreach, so the practical goal is improving early-warning precision and recall rather than only overall AUC. Better means catching more true dropouts with fewer false alerts before the intervention window closes.
design_challenges: The data are temporally structured, so the experiment must use chronological train/validation/test splits to avoid leakage from future course cohorts or later-in-course behavior.

Class imbalance and label ambiguity are likely, because dropout may be rare in some courses and may be confounded with legitimate completion patterns such as auditing, late withdrawals, or transfer between course sections.
known_confounds: Any performance gain could be driven by course-level difficulty, instructor differences, or semester-specific platform changes rather than the sequence features themselves, so a critic would check stratification or cohort-level effects.

hypothesis: A transformer-based model trained on historical SKU-level sales, promotion, price, and stockout signals will forecast 14-day demand more accurately than a gradient-boosted tree baseline built on lagged summary features for a multi-region retail inventory system, especially during holiday and promotion periods.
domain: Retail inventory management and demand forecasting for a multi-region consumer goods chain
available_data: A retailer would typically have daily SKU-store sales, prices, promotions, inventory levels, stockouts, and calendar/holiday flags for several thousand SKUs across dozens to hundreds of stores over multiple years. Labels are naturally available as realized future sales or demand, though true demand may need to be inferred from censored sales during stockouts. The data also often includes assortment changes, new product launches, and regional differences that create realistic variation.
success_metric_context: Operations cares about reducing stockouts, excess inventory, and waste while maintaining service levels. Better performance means more accurate forecasts at the SKU-store-horizon level, especially for high-volume and promotion-sensitive items where forecasting errors have the largest financial impact.
design_challenges: The time structure requires chronological evaluation with rolling-origin backtesting rather than random splits, because leakage from future promotions or demand regime shifts would otherwise inflate results.

Observed sales are censored by stockouts and intermittent availability, so the experiment must decide whether to model realized sales or latent demand and handle sparse, intermittent series differently from fast-moving items.
known_confounds: Any apparent gain could be driven by better handling of calendar effects, stockout censoring, or new-product sparsity rather than the transformer architecture itself, so a critic would check whether the comparator received equivalent feature access and horizon-specific tuning.

hypothesis: A gradient-boosted model trained on event-level transaction sequences with merchant, device, and velocity features will detect first-party card fraud better than a static tabular baseline trained on aggregated account features, as measured by PR-AUC on future transactions from a later time period.
domain: Finance / card payments fraud detection for a consumer issuer processing real-time card transactions
available_data: A card issuer would realistically have millions of labeled authorization events over multiple months, including transaction amount, merchant category, device fingerprint, geolocation, timestamp, account history, and chargeback/fraud confirmation labels that arrive with delay. The dataset would naturally include severe class imbalance and evolving fraud patterns, with labels only partially observed at prediction time due to reporting lag.
success_metric_context: The operational goal is to catch more fraudulent transactions before authorization or shortly after while minimizing false declines that annoy legitimate cardholders. Better performance means higher precision at a fixed recall, or higher PR-AUC, on truly out-of-time future transactions rather than random splits.
design_challenges: The data has strong temporal dependence and label delay, so evaluation must use a chronological split that prevents leakage from future fraud patterns into training.
Fraud behavior drifts over time and varies by merchant type and geography, so the experiment must account for distribution shift and potential confounding from seasonality or policy changes.
known_confounds: Any apparent gain could come from the sequence model simply exploiting more recent training data, better calibration, or leakage through post-transaction features rather than from using sequential structure itself.

hypothesis: In a large retail fraud-detection setting, a sequence model trained on the ordered transaction history for each cardholder will detect first-party and account-takeover fraud more effectively than a feature-aggregated gradient-boosted baseline, as measured by fraud recall at a fixed investigator-review rate.
domain: Finance / card payments fraud detection in a large consumer bank or card network
available_data: Transaction logs would realistically exist for millions of card transactions over multiple months or years, including timestamp, merchant, amount, channel, device and location signals, and historical account/card metadata. Fraud labels are usually available with delay from chargebacks, customer disputes, or analyst investigation outcomes, creating a labeled dataset with substantial class imbalance and delayed-positive examples.
success_metric_context: The business goal is to catch more true fraud cases without overwhelming analysts or blocking legitimate purchases. Better performance means higher recall or precision at the same fixed manual-review budget, not just higher overall AUC.
design_challenges: Transactions are temporally ordered and fraud patterns evolve, so the evaluation must use a chronological split rather than random shuffling to avoid leakage and overstated gains.

Fraud labels are delayed and incomplete, and class imbalance is extreme, so the experiment must handle label lag and choose an operational metric aligned with review capacity rather than raw accuracy.
known_confounds: Any improvement could be driven by access to recency or merchant-history signals rather than the sequence architecture itself, so a critic would need to check whether the baseline was given the same temporal features and labeling window.

hypothesis: A two-tower retrieval model that incorporates recent user session behavior and item text embeddings will retrieve relevant training videos more effectively than a collaborative-filtering-only baseline for recommending next-course content on an online learning platform, as measured by held-out engagement and completion outcomes.
domain: Media / content recommendation for an online learning platform with clickstream, watch history, and course metadata
available_data: An online learning platform would realistically have timestamped clickstream logs, video watch events, course enrollments, search queries, dwell time, and completion records for hundreds of thousands of learners over multiple semesters or months. It would also have item-side metadata such as titles, descriptions, tags, instructor information, difficulty level, and transcript-derived text embeddings, with labels implicitly available from what learners click, watch, save, and complete.
success_metric_context: The stakeholder cares about whether recommended content increases meaningful learning behavior, not just clicks. Better means higher downstream watch completion, course progression, and return engagement, while avoiding recommending overly popular but low-completion items.
design_challenges: User behavior is highly temporal and session-dependent, so the evaluation must separate recent intent from long-run preference and avoid leakage from future interactions.

Exposure bias and popularity bias can dominate offline metrics in recommendation logs, making it necessary to reason carefully about counterfactual evaluation and whether improved ranking reflects true relevance or just amplifying already popular content.
known_confounds: Any apparent gain could be driven by popularity signals, catalog freshness, or differences in item cold-start frequency rather than the session-aware text representation itself.

hypothesis: For hospital coding, a sequence model fine-tuned on the full clinical note text will predict the next-encounter ICD-10 diagnosis set more accurately than a bag-of-words TF-IDF baseline built from the same notes, especially for rare codes and multi-code encounters.
domain: Healthcare / clinical NLP for ICD-10 diagnosis coding from discharge summaries and progress notes in a hospital billing workflow
available_data: A realistic dataset would consist of de-identified inpatient discharge summaries, selected progress notes, and the final billed ICD-10 diagnosis codes for each encounter from a multi-hospital EHR system. Hospitals commonly have hundreds of thousands of encounters across several years, with notes written at discharge and labels available after coding is finalized; code frequencies are typically highly imbalanced and many encounters contain multiple labels.
success_metric_context: Hospital coding teams care about accurate code assignment because it affects reimbursement, quality reporting, and downstream analytics. Better performance means improving macro-F1 and precision/recall on rare diagnosis codes without materially hurting performance on common codes or increasing false positives that create manual coder workload.
design_challenges: The label space is multi-label and extremely imbalanced, so the evaluation must account for both frequent and rare ICD-10 codes rather than relying only on micro-averaged accuracy.

Clinical notes are temporally structured and may include information from multiple note types; a careful experimenter must decide whether to use only pre-discharge text, how to handle note concatenation order, and how to prevent leakage from post-discharge documentation.
known_confounds: Any improvement could be driven by longer-context modeling rather than the sequence architecture itself, or by inadvertent leakage from coding-related phrases in notes that appear after the coding decision.

hypothesis: In a cyber threat-detection setting, a sequence model trained on ordered endpoint telemetry events will detect novel intrusion incidents better than a tabular gradient-boosted baseline built from aggregated count features, as measured by event-level AUROC and early-warning recall at a fixed false-positive rate.
domain: Cybersecurity / enterprise endpoint intrusion detection using endpoint detection and response (EDR) telemetry
available_data: Enterprise security teams typically retain timestamped EDR logs for hosts over months or years, including process launches, command-line arguments, network connections, file modifications, and authentication events, along with incident-response tickets that mark confirmed intrusion windows. A realistic dataset would contain millions of events across thousands of endpoints, with labels derived from analyst-confirmed alerts, threat-hunting findings, and post-incident investigations. The data are highly imbalanced, with far fewer confirmed attacks than benign activity, and labels may be noisy or delayed relative to the first malicious event.
success_metric_context: Security operations teams care about identifying attacks early enough to contain them while keeping analyst workload manageable. Better performance means catching more true incidents sooner at an acceptable false-positive rate, not just maximizing overall classification accuracy.
design_challenges: The data are temporally ordered and labels often correspond to incident windows rather than single events, so evaluation must respect chronology and avoid leakage across the same host or campaign.

Class imbalance is extreme and benign activity is heterogeneous across departments and software stacks, so the experiment must account for calibration and the operational cost of false alerts versus missed intrusions.
known_confounds: Any improvement could be driven by the sequence model exploiting host- or user-specific activity patterns, differences in feature engineering, or leakage from post-compromise artifacts rather than genuinely better detection of early malicious behavior.

hypothesis: In a consumer video streaming platform, a two-tower neural recommender that incorporates recent session sequence embeddings and time-of-day context will improve next-item click-through rate and 7-day watch-time retention more than a collaborative-filtering baseline trained only on historical user-item interactions.
domain: Media / content recommendation for a subscription video streaming service with session-level viewing logs
available_data: The platform would realistically have several months of anonymized user-item interaction logs, including impressions, clicks, watch duration, session timestamps, device type, and content metadata such as genre, language, and recency of release. Labels are implicit feedback derived from impressions and subsequent clicks or watch events, with enough volume to support train/validation/test splits by time and user cohort.
success_metric_context: The stakeholder cares about recommending titles that users actually start and continue watching, not just maximizing clicks on popular content. Better performance means higher click-through on impressions without degrading downstream watch time or short-term retention, especially for returning users with evolving tastes.
design_challenges: The data are strongly time-dependent, so evaluation must use chronological splits to avoid leakage from future preferences and content popularity spikes.
Implicit-feedback recommendation is affected by exposure bias and popularity bias, so a careful comparison must distinguish true personalization gains from recommending already-overexposed titles.
known_confounds: Any observed improvement could be driven by better use of fresh popularity trends or content metadata rather than session sequence modeling itself, so a critic would check whether gains persist under matched exposure and freshness controls.

hypothesis: For AML transaction monitoring at a large retail bank, a sequence model over each customer’s recent transaction history and merchant/category embeddings will detect confirmed suspicious activity better than a tabular gradient-boosted baseline on aggregated per-account features, as measured by precision at a fixed low alert rate.
domain: Finance / anti-money-laundering transaction monitoring for retail banking customers
available_data: A bank would realistically have several years of transaction records with timestamps, amounts, merchant descriptors, geographic location, channel, and account metadata, plus investigator dispositions on alerts and a smaller set of confirmed suspicious cases from SAR/STR outcomes or internal adjudication. The data is naturally imbalanced, with labels often delayed and only partially observed for some accounts, and historical transactions can be grouped into rolling monthly snapshots for model training and evaluation.
success_metric_context: The stakeholder cares about finding the small number of truly suspicious accounts that investigators should review, while keeping false positives low enough that analyst capacity is not overwhelmed. Better performance means higher precision among the top-scored alerts at a constrained review budget, not just higher overall accuracy or AUC.
design_challenges: The label is delayed and incomplete, so the evaluation split must respect time ordering and avoid leakage from future investigations or post-alert behavior.

Suspicious activity patterns are sparse and heterogeneous, so the experiment must handle extreme class imbalance and account for customer-level clustering to avoid overestimating gains from sequence modeling.
known_confounds: Any improvement could be driven by recency effects, richer merchant/category encoding, or differences in how often high-risk customers were historically investigated rather than by the sequence model itself.

hypothesis: A transformer-based semantic retrieval model fine-tuned on biomedical passage relevance judgments will retrieve the correct gene-disease or protein-function abstract more effectively than a TF-IDF/BM25 baseline for a literature search task in scientific research, measured by nDCG@10 and recall@100.
domain: Scientific research / biomedical literature retrieval for gene-disease and protein-function evidence discovery
available_data: PubMed abstracts and titles, along with relevance judgments from curated biomedical search benchmarks and expert annotation sets, would realistically exist for this task. The corpus can span millions of abstracts over many years, with query sets built from gene, disease, pathway, or function queries and graded relevance labels indicating whether an abstract is directly relevant, partially relevant, or irrelevant.
success_metric_context: Researchers care about finding the most relevant evidence quickly, especially highly relevant papers that support annotation, hypothesis generation, or systematic review. Better performance means fewer missed relevant abstracts in the top results and higher-quality ranking of truly useful evidence near the top of the list.
design_challenges: Queries are often short, ambiguous, and synonym-heavy, so the evaluation must account for semantic matching rather than exact keyword overlap, while preserving a fair comparison to lexical retrieval.

Label scarcity and grading bias are important: relevance judgments are expensive and usually incomplete, so the experiment must handle partial pooling of judged documents and avoid overstating gains from a model that simply exploits annotation artifacts.
known_confounds: Observed gains could be driven by document-length priors, vocabulary overlap with training queries, or benchmark leakage from overlapping abstracts rather than true semantic retrieval improvements.

hypothesis: In hospital oncology workflows, a transformer-based semantic search model fine-tuned on clinician query–note relevance pairs will retrieve the correct prior pathology and treatment documents more accurately than a BM25 keyword baseline, improving top-5 retrieval success for downstream chart review.
domain: Healthcare / clinical NLP — semantic retrieval for oncology chart review from longitudinal EHR notes and pathology reports
available_data: A realistic dataset would consist of de-identified oncology patient records from a large hospital system, including pathology reports, progress notes, discharge summaries, and clinician search queries used during chart review or tumor board prep. Labels could come from which documents were clicked, opened, or marked relevant by clinicians over 2–5 years of workflow logs, yielding tens of thousands of query-document pairs with strong temporal ordering and sparse positives.
success_metric_context: The stakeholder goal is to reduce time spent finding the right prior evidence in the chart and improve the chance that a clinician sees the most relevant documents within the first few results. Better means fewer missed relevant records and less manual searching during chart review, not just higher offline retrieval scores.
design_challenges: The data are temporally ordered and query intent may drift as documentation templates and oncology terminology change, so evaluation should respect chronological splits rather than random splits.

Relevance labels are likely implicit and noisy because clicks depend on document position, note availability, and clinician workflow, so the experiment must account for position bias and partial feedback.
known_confounds: Any improvement could be driven by the transformer learning common note templates or date proximity rather than true semantic matching, so a critic would check whether gains persist after controlling for document length, recency, and query vocabulary overlap.

hypothesis: For hospital pathology workflows, a transformer model that conditions on both whole-slide image embeddings and structured tumor pathology attributes will predict 2-year recurrence-free survival more accurately than a model using structured pathology attributes alone.
domain: Healthcare / clinical ML — oncology prognostication from digital pathology and structured pathology reports
available_data: A tertiary cancer center would typically have digital pathology slides from resected tumors, synoptic pathology reports with structured variables such as grade, margin status, lymphovascular invasion, and stage, plus longitudinal oncology follow-up capturing recurrence dates and censoring. The cohort could span several years and include thousands to tens of thousands of cases across one or more tumor types, with outcome labels available through the EHR and cancer registry. Slide scans and report text/fields are naturally paired at the case level, but follow-up length is variable and many patients are censored before 2 years.
success_metric_context: Clinicians care about identifying high-risk patients early enough to guide adjuvant therapy intensity and surveillance scheduling. Better means improved discrimination for recurrence risk over a fixed horizon while remaining calibrated enough to support patient-level triage decisions.
design_challenges: The outcome is time-to-event with censoring, so the experiment must use survival-aware evaluation rather than simple accuracy and must handle patients with incomplete follow-up.
Whole-slide images are large and noisy, and the model must avoid leakage from slide scanner/site artifacts or from multiple specimens from the same patient appearing across train and test splits.
known_confounds: Any apparent gain may be driven by tumor subtype or institution-specific scanning/reporting patterns rather than true histomorphologic signal, so a careful critic would check site- and stage-stratified performance and external validity.

hypothesis: A sequence model trained on raw purchase and browsing event streams will predict 30-day customer churn more accurately than a model built from aggregated recency-frequency-monetary features and static demographics for a subscription-based retail app.
domain: E-commerce / subscription retail customer churn prediction from clickstream and transaction logs
available_data: A subscription retail company would typically have several years of user-level event logs including app opens, product views, add-to-cart events, purchases, cancellations, and customer service interactions, along with account attributes and billing history. Labels can be defined retrospectively as churn within 30 days of a reference date, and the dataset would naturally be time-stamped at the event level with millions of user-months but a much smaller positive churn rate.
success_metric_context: The stakeholder cares about identifying customers at risk of canceling in time to target retention offers efficiently, so improvement should be measured in terms of ranking quality or recall at a fixed contact budget, not just overall accuracy. A better model is one that surfaces more true churners among the top-scored customers while avoiding unnecessary incentives to loyal users.
design_challenges: The data has strong temporal dependence, so evaluation must use a chronological split to avoid leakage from future behavior into training features or labels.

Churn is typically highly imbalanced and may be affected by marketing interventions, so the experiment must separate genuine predictive signal from treatment effects and choose an evaluation metric robust to class imbalance.
known_confounds: Any observed gain could be driven by the sequence model capturing more recent activity or longer observation windows rather than truly modeling behavior sequences, so a critic would check feature parity and window alignment across baselines.

hypothesis: For subscription video streaming, a sequence model that uses the last 30 days of session-level viewing logs and context features (time of day, device, playback completion, and recency) will predict next-week churn better than a static user-profile baseline built from aggregate lifetime counts and demographics.
domain: Media / content subscription video streaming churn prediction from session-level viewing behavior
available_data: A streaming service would realistically have per-user session logs covering timestamps, titles watched, watch duration, completion rate, device type, and app entry source, along with account-level metadata such as plan type and tenure. Churn labels are naturally available from subscription cancellations or non-renewals, and the data could span multiple months to years with millions of users and highly imbalanced churn events.
success_metric_context: The stakeholder cares about identifying users likely to cancel soon enough to trigger retention interventions, so precision at the top of the ranked list and recall on true churners matter more than raw accuracy. A better model is one that improves early warning for high-value users while keeping false positives low enough that retention offers are not wasted.
design_challenges: The data are temporally structured, so evaluation must avoid leakage by using chronological splits and defining the prediction window and label window carefully.
Churn is typically rare and seasonality can be strong around holidays, major content releases, or billing cycles, which makes calibration and distribution shift important to handle.
known_confounds: Any observed gain could come from the sequence model simply exploiting more recent activity volume or tenure effects rather than true behavioral patterns, so a critic would check whether the baseline was given comparable recency features and whether performance holds across user cohorts and seasons.

hypothesis: For a subscription music streaming service, a session-aware sequential recommendation model trained on user listening sequences and context (time of day, device, session recency) will outperform a two-tower collaborative filtering baseline on next-track recommendation, measured by ranking quality on held-out future sessions.
domain: Media / content recommendation for a subscription music streaming service with session-level listening logs
available_data: The service would realistically have anonymized event logs of millions of listens over months or years, including track plays, skips, likes, playlist adds, session boundaries, timestamps, device type, and coarse user attributes. Labels can be constructed from implicit feedback by treating the next played track, completion, or skip behavior as the target signal, with new sessions and future weeks held out for evaluation.
success_metric_context: The stakeholder cares about surfacing the next song the listener is likely to actually play and continue listening to, because that affects session length, skip rate, and retention. Better performance means more relevant recommendations in real usage, not just higher offline hit rate on easy popularity-driven items.
design_challenges: The data are temporally ordered and highly non-stationary, so the evaluation must use chronological splits to avoid leakage from future listening trends and new releases.
Implicit feedback is biased by exposure and position, so the experiment must distinguish true preference gains from the model simply recommending popular or heavily promoted tracks.
known_confounds: Any observed improvement could be driven by popularity bias, catalog freshness, or better calibration to session length rather than the sequential modeling itself.

hypothesis: For subscription video streaming, a session-aware sequence model trained on per-session viewing logs and short-term context will improve next-item recommendation quality over a static collaborative-filtering baseline using only aggregated user-item interaction history, especially for users with sparse long-term histories.
domain: Media / content recommendation for a subscription video streaming service with session-level viewing logs
available_data: A streaming platform would realistically have timestamped viewing events, session boundaries, titles watched, watch duration, device type, and content metadata over many months or years for millions of users and thousands of items. Labels can be derived from implicit feedback such as the next title clicked, watched to completion, or abandoned, with separate holdout periods available for offline evaluation.

design_challenges: The data are strongly temporally structured and extremely imbalanced, so the evaluation must avoid leakage across time and likely needs a chronological split rather than random shuffling.

Attack techniques evolve, so performance should be assessed not only on held-out hosts but also on later time periods or unseen threat families to test whether the model generalizes beyond memorized signatures.
known_confounds: Any gain from sequence modeling could be driven by the model exploiting host identity, environment-specific baselines, or label artifacts from the alerting pipeline rather than learning genuinely better malicious-behavior patterns.

hypothesis: For customer support analytics in a B2B SaaS platform, a transformer model fine-tuned on ticket text plus CRM interaction history will predict whether a ticket escalates to a manager better than a model trained on ticket text alone, measured on future tickets from unseen weeks.
domain: Customer support analytics for a B2B SaaS platform using support tickets, chat transcripts, and CRM interaction logs
available_data: A typical SaaS company would have historical support tickets, live chat transcripts, account metadata, and CRM event logs spanning many months or years, with labels such as escalation, reopen, or manager assignment recorded by the support workflow. The dataset would naturally include tens of thousands to millions of tickets, but only a minority would escalate, and the same customer or account may appear repeatedly over time.
success_metric_context: Support leaders care about catching likely escalations early so they can route issues to the right tier and reduce time-to-resolution and customer frustration. Better performance means higher recall and precision on truly escalated cases, not just a small gain in overall accuracy.
design_challenges: The data are temporally structured and customer-linked, so evaluation should respect chronology and avoid leakage from future interactions or the same account appearing in both train and test without careful grouping.
Escalations are likely class-imbalanced and partially triggered by policy changes or staffing shifts, making threshold choice and metric selection non-trivial across changing support workflows.
known_confounds: Any observed improvement could come from extra metadata in CRM logs, longer ticket histories, or leakage from post-escalation signals rather than from the richer representation itself, so a careful critic would check feature timing and account-level overlap.

hypothesis: For a subscription video streaming service, a sequence model trained on session-level viewing histories and recent engagement context will predict 7-day churn more accurately than a static aggregate-feature baseline using only lifetime usage summaries, as measured by out-of-time AUPRC.
domain: Media / subscription video streaming churn prediction from session-level viewing behavior
available_data: The dataset would realistically come from a streaming platform’s event logs: timestamps for plays, pauses, stops, rewinds, content metadata, device type, session duration, and account tenure over several months to a few years. Labels can be defined from cancellation or inactivity observed within 7 days after a prediction cutoff, with many more non-churn than churn examples and strong seasonality around releases and holidays.
success_metric_context: The stakeholder cares about identifying at-risk subscribers early enough to trigger retention interventions without overwhelming the CRM or offering unnecessary incentives. Better means higher precision on the highest-risk users and more true churners captured before they leave, not just a marginal lift in overall accuracy.
design_challenges: The data is temporally ordered, so the evaluation must use chronological splits to avoid leakage from future viewing behavior into training.
Churn is typically rare and behavior changes with content releases and holidays, so class imbalance and distribution shift can materially affect which model appears better.
known_confounds: Any apparent gain from the sequence model could be driven by access to more granular recency information rather than true sequential pattern learning, so a critic would check whether a recency-matched baseline closes the gap.

hypothesis: For airline cancellation risk prediction, a gradient-boosted model trained on flight-history and airport-operations features augmented with weather-interaction summaries will outperform a baseline that uses only scheduled flight and static route features, as measured by AUROC and calibration on future unseen months.
domain: Transportation / aviation operations — airline cancellation risk prediction from flight schedules, airport operations, and weather feeds
available_data: A commercial airline or aviation-operations dataset would realistically include historical flight legs, scheduled departure/arrival times, airport identifiers, aircraft tail numbers, delay/cancellation outcomes, airport congestion indicators, and contemporaneous weather feeds (e.g., visibility, wind, precipitation, storms) over multiple years. Labels are typically available from flight-operations records, with cancellations and diversions recorded per flight leg and timestamps enabling chronological evaluation.
success_metric_context: Operations teams care about identifying flights likely to be canceled early enough to rebook passengers, reposition crews, and adjust gate/aircraft assignments. Better means not just higher discrimination, but reliable probabilities that can support threshold-based interventions without generating too many false alerts.
design_challenges: The data is strongly time-dependent, so evaluation must avoid leakage by using a strict chronological split across seasons and major weather events.

Cancellations are relatively rare and can cluster during disruptions, so the experiment must account for class imbalance and event correlation across airports and days; otherwise apparent gains may come from predicting broad disruption days rather than individual flight risk.
known_confounds: Any improvement could be driven by better capture of seasonality or airport-specific cancellation rates rather than the weather-interaction features themselves, so a careful critic would compare against a route/airport/time baseline and inspect calibration by airport and month.

hypothesis: A gradient-boosted survival model trained on pathology-derived tumor morphology features plus structured cancer registry variables will predict overall survival better than a model using registry variables alone for resected colorectal cancer patients.
domain: Healthcare / clinical ML — oncology prognostication from digital pathology and structured cancer registry data
available_data: Hospital cancer registries and pathology systems would realistically contain patient demographics, stage, grade, treatment dates, recurrence indicators, and overall survival outcomes for thousands of colorectal cancer cases over multiple years. Whole-slide pathology images or precomputed morphology features from resection specimens could be linked to registry records, with labels available via follow-up and mortality tracking.
success_metric_context: The stakeholder cares about identifying high-risk patients who may need closer surveillance or adjuvant treatment discussions after surgery. Better performance means improved discrimination and clinically useful risk stratification on held-out future patients, especially for earlier-than-expected deaths.
design_challenges: The outcome is time-to-event with censoring, so the comparison must use survival-aware metrics and not reduce the problem to a naive binary label at a fixed horizon.

Pathology and registry data may come from different sites or eras, creating batch effects and treatment-era shift that can inflate apparent gains unless the evaluation split is temporally and/or institutionally separated.
known_confounds: Any observed improvement could be driven by the pathology model capturing tumor stage proxies or site-specific scanner artifacts rather than true biological signal, so a careful critic would check calibration and robustness across institutions and time periods.

hypothesis: For enterprise endpoint intrusion detection, a sequence model trained on short windows of raw EDR event streams will detect novel malware more effectively than a tabular model built from aggregated host-level counts and summary statistics, especially when evaluated on a temporally held-out month with previously unseen attack families.
domain: Cybersecurity / enterprise endpoint intrusion detection using endpoint detection and response (EDR) telemetry from corporate Windows and Linux endpoints
available_data: Large organizations commonly retain endpoint detection and response logs with process creation, command-line arguments, file writes, registry edits, network connections, parent-child process relationships, and alert outcomes. These logs can span months to years across thousands to millions of endpoint sessions, with incident-response labels derived from analyst triage, malware confirmations, and threat-hunting investigations. The data is naturally time-stamped and highly imbalanced, with relatively few confirmed malicious sequences compared with abundant benign activity.
success_metric_context: Security teams care about catching true intrusions early without overwhelming analysts with false positives. Better performance means higher precision at a fixed low false-positive rate, or higher recall on confirmed malicious activity while preserving alert volume within operational capacity.
design_challenges: The data is temporally structured, so a chronological split is needed to avoid leakage from future attack patterns into training and to test generalization to new campaigns.

Class imbalance and label delay are severe, because many malicious episodes are only confirmed after investigation and may be partially observed, making evaluation sensitive to how positive windows are defined.
known_confounds: Any lift could come from the sequence model exploiting host-specific metadata, event volume, or labeling artifacts rather than learning attacker behavior, so a careful critic would check robustness across endpoint types, business units, and attack families.

hypothesis: For airline cancellation risk prediction, a model that incorporates airport-specific temporal context and weather lag features from the prior 6-24 hours will outperform the same model trained only on scheduled flight and static route metadata in AUROC and calibration on a future-month holdout.
domain: Transportation / aviation operations — airline cancellation risk prediction from flight schedules, airport operations, and weather feeds
available_data: A commercial airline or aviation operations dataset would plausibly contain scheduled flight records, aircraft tail assignments, airport operational status, historical weather observations/forecasts, and actual cancellation outcomes over multiple months or years. Labels are naturally available from flight operations logs, while covariates can be assembled at booking time or shortly before departure from internal dispatch systems and public weather feeds. The data would likely be highly imbalanced, with cancellations representing a minority of flights and strong seasonality across airports, carriers, and weather events.
success_metric_context: The operational goal is to identify flights at elevated cancellation risk early enough to rebook passengers, reposition aircraft, and allocate gate/crew resources. Better performance means not just higher discrimination, but reliable probability estimates that can support threshold-based intervention policies without overwhelming operations teams with false alarms.
design_challenges: The data has strong temporal dependence and seasonality, so evaluation must respect chronology rather than random splitting to avoid leakage from future weather patterns or operational disruptions.

Rare-event imbalance and airport heterogeneity make it non-trivial to compare models fairly; the experiment must account for whether gains come from genuinely useful lagged context or from overfitting to a few high-disruption airports or storm periods.
known_confounds: Any observed improvement could be driven by access to more recent operational signals rather than the temporal context itself, or by differences in how missing weather/airport-status fields are handled across routes and carriers.

hypothesis: For a B2B SaaS support team, a transformer-based classifier trained on the full text of support tickets, chat transcripts, and prior CRM interactions will identify escalation-prone tickets more accurately than a baseline using only engineered metadata features, when evaluated on future tickets from the same customers and products.
domain: Customer support analytics for a B2B SaaS platform using support tickets, chat transcripts, and CRM interaction logs
available_data: A typical SaaS company would have thousands to millions of historical support tickets, live chat transcripts, and CRM records spanning multiple years, with timestamps, product area tags, customer account metadata, agent assignments, and resolution outcomes. Escalation labels could be derived from whether a ticket was routed to Tier 2/engineering, reopened multiple times, or breached an SLA threshold. The data are naturally temporal and include both structured fields and unstructured language from customers and agents.
success_metric_context: The operational goal is to catch risky tickets early so managers can prioritize routing, reduce SLA breaches, and avoid customer churn. In practice, better means higher precision-recall performance on rare escalations, not just raw accuracy, because false alarms consume scarce agent time.
design_challenges: The target is likely imbalanced and temporally autocorrelated, so the evaluation must avoid leakage by splitting chronologically rather than randomly.

Customer-level and product-level dependence can inflate performance if the same accounts or recurring issue templates appear in both train and test, so the experiment must decide whether to generalize to new tickets, new customers, or both.
known_confounds: Any gain from the text model could be partly explained by longer or more detailed tickets, product-specific jargon, or agent-response patterns rather than true semantic understanding of escalation risk.

hypothesis: For enterprise endpoint intrusion detection on corporate Windows and Linux telemetry, a sequence model trained on raw event timelines and process trees will detect novel intrusions with higher PR-AUC than a tabular baseline built from aggregated per-host summary features.
domain: Cybersecurity / enterprise endpoint intrusion detection using endpoint detection and response (EDR) telemetry from corporate Windows and Linux endpoints
available_data: Typical EDR data would include process creation events, command lines, file writes, network connections, registry edits, parent-child process relationships, and host/user metadata collected continuously across thousands of endpoints over months. Labels would come from SOC-confirmed incidents, malware sandbox verdicts, or threat-hunting case closures, with most windows unlabeled or weakly labeled and attacks being rare. A realistic dataset would be highly imbalanced and temporally ordered, with concept drift as attacker techniques and software baselines change.
success_metric_context: Security teams care about surfacing true intrusions early while keeping analyst workload manageable, so improvements should be judged by precision-recall performance and alert volume at a fixed review budget. A better model is one that finds more confirmed malicious activity without materially increasing false positives on benign enterprise activity.
design_challenges: The data are temporally dependent, so evaluation must avoid leakage by using chronological splits and ensuring future host activity does not inform training on earlier incidents.
Alert labels are incomplete and delayed, and many attacks are only partially observed, so the experiment must define a consistent labeling window and handle weak supervision / censoring carefully.
known_confounds: Any observed gain could come from the sequence model exploiting longer observation windows or richer host metadata rather than temporal structure itself, so a critic would check whether the comparison is matched on input horizon, feature availability, and label latency.

hypothesis: For enterprise endpoint intrusion detection, a sequence model trained on per-endpoint event timelines and cross-session behavior patterns will outperform a tabular model built from aggregated 24-hour EDR summary features at identifying malicious activity, especially for low-and-slow attacks.
domain: Cybersecurity / enterprise endpoint intrusion detection using endpoint detection and response (EDR) telemetry from corporate Windows and Linux endpoints
available_data: A large enterprise SOC would realistically have months of EDR telemetry including process starts, command lines, parent-child process trees, network connections, file writes, registry edits, login events, and detection analyst labels for confirmed incidents or benign activity. The data would be naturally timestamped, highly imbalanced, and span many endpoints, users, and software versions, with labels arriving after analyst triage or incident response. Historical alerts and case outcomes would provide a supervised signal, though positive examples would be sparse and delayed.
success_metric_context: The operational goal is to reduce missed intrusions without overwhelming analysts with false alerts, so the main metric should reflect ranking or precision at a very low false-positive rate rather than raw accuracy. In practice, the security team cares about how many true malicious endpoints or sessions are surfaced early enough to investigate and contain.
design_challenges: Temporal leakage is a major risk because many EDR features can accidentally encode post-incident behavior; the evaluation needs strict chronological splitting and event-time cutoff rules.

Class imbalance and label noise are severe because most alerts are benign and some attacks are only partially labeled; the experiment must account for incomplete ground truth and delayed analyst confirmation.
known_confounds: Any improvement from a sequence model could be driven by richer feature count rather than temporal reasoning, or by leakage from endpoint-specific artifacts, software inventory, or alert-generation rules that differ across time and hosts.

hypothesis: For a streaming music service, a session-aware sequence model trained on the order and timing of listening events will predict 7-day subscription churn more accurately than an aggregate feature baseline built from counts, recency, and genre preferences, especially for users with short or irregular listening histories.
domain: Media / content streaming churn prediction for a subscription music service using session-level listening logs
available_data: A subscription music platform would realistically have longitudinal user-level listening logs with timestamps, track/artist/playlist identifiers, skips, replays, session boundaries, device type, and subscription status changes over multiple months or years. Labels can be constructed from whether a user cancels within 7 days after a prediction point, with tens of thousands to millions of user-weeks available and strong class imbalance because churn is relatively rare in any given week.
success_metric_context: The business objective is to identify at-risk subscribers early enough to trigger retention interventions, so practical value comes from ranking true churners near the top under limited outreach capacity. Better performance means higher precision/recall tradeoff at the intervention threshold and improved lift over the baseline among users most likely to receive a retention offer.
design_challenges: The data are temporally structured, so evaluation needs a chronological split to avoid leakage from future listening patterns and post-treatment behavior.
Churn is both imbalanced and confounded by tenure and seasonality, so the experiment must control for user age, promotional periods, and returning-vs-new subscriber behavior when comparing models.
known_confounds: Any apparent gain from the sequence model could be driven by greater sensitivity to tenure or activity volume rather than true sequential information, so a careful critic would check whether the improvement remains after matching on history length and recent engagement level.

hypothesis: In subscription music streaming, a sequence model trained on session-level listening history plus recent skips and dwell-time signals will predict 30-day churn better than an aggregate-feature logistic regression baseline, especially for users with short and irregular listening sessions.
domain: Media / content streaming churn prediction for a subscription music service using session-level listening logs
available_data: A music streaming platform would realistically have timestamped listening logs, play/skipped events, dwell time, device type, artist/track identifiers, playlist context, and subscription status for millions of users over many months. Churn labels can be derived from whether a subscriber cancels or becomes inactive within the next 30 days after a prediction date, with historical snapshots available for training and evaluation. The data is naturally longitudinal and includes both high-frequency session behavior and sparse user-level outcomes.
success_metric_context: The stakeholder cares about identifying at-risk subscribers early enough to trigger retention offers or product interventions without wasting incentives on stable users. Better performance means higher ranking quality on churn risk at operationally relevant thresholds, not just overall accuracy, because interventions are targeted to a small fraction of the user base.
design_challenges: The data has strong temporal dependence, so the evaluation must avoid leakage by using chronological train/validation/test splits and by defining each example at a fixed prediction snapshot.

Churn is usually imbalanced and can be confounded by seasonality or recent product changes, so the experiment must distinguish true behavioral signal from time-based drift and changing retention policies.
known_confounds: Any lift could be explained by the sequence model capturing recency or session length more effectively rather than richer temporal dynamics, so a careful critic would compare against a well-tuned baseline with the same raw inputs and controlled history window.

hypothesis: A sequence model trained on raw courier event streams with weather and dispatch context will predict late-delivery risk more accurately than a gradient-boosted model built from manually aggregated delivery features for last-mile food delivery orders.
domain: Transportation / logistics — last-mile food delivery late-delivery risk prediction from courier telemetry and dispatch logs
available_data: A food-delivery platform would typically have millions of historical orders with timestamps for order placement, restaurant acceptance, courier assignment, pickup, and drop-off, plus courier GPS pings, route traces, dispatch events, order attributes, and local weather/traffic feeds. Labels can be defined from whether an order exceeded a promised delivery SLA or a lateness threshold, with data naturally spanning multiple cities and seasons over months or years.
success_metric_context: The operational goal is to identify orders at high risk of lateness early enough to intervene with re-dispatching, customer communication, or rerouting. Better performance means fewer late orders and better prioritization at the same alert volume, not just higher offline AUC.
design_challenges: The data are temporally structured and decision-dependent, so evaluation must respect chronological splits and avoid leakage from future courier or route information.
Late deliveries are typically a minority class and vary by city, restaurant, and weather regime, so the experiment must handle imbalance and distribution shift across geography and time.
known_confounds: Any gain could be driven by the sequence model exploiting finer-grained proxy signals such as courier idiosyncrasies or post-dispatch route drift rather than truly learning better spatiotemporal dynamics of delay risk.

hypothesis: For airline cancellation risk prediction, a gradient-boosted model trained on temporally engineered airport- and route-level features plus weather forecasts will outperform a model trained only on flight schedule fields, with the largest gain on disruption-heavy winter months.
domain: Transportation / aviation operations — airline cancellation risk prediction from flight schedules, airport operations, and weather feeds
available_data: A major airline or flight operations team would realistically have historical flight schedules, actual departure/arrival outcomes, gate and airport congestion indicators, aircraft rotations, and contemporaneous weather feeds for each flight leg. Labels such as canceled versus operated are naturally available from operational records over multiple years, with enough volume to support seasonal and route-specific splits.
success_metric_context: The operational goal is to identify high-cancellation-risk flights early enough to support rebooking, crew, and gate planning. Better performance means higher precision and recall on cancellations under a time-based evaluation, especially during periods when disruptions are common and false alarms are costly.
design_challenges: The data are strongly time-dependent, so evaluation must use chronological splits to avoid leakage from future weather, downstream cancellations, or season-specific patterns.

Cancellation is typically rare and geographically clustered, so class imbalance and route/airport heterogeneity can make aggregate metrics misleading unless performance is examined across seasons and airports.
known_confounds: Any observed improvement could be driven by the model simply learning seasonal weather proxies or airport-specific delay patterns rather than genuinely leveraging richer operational context, so a critic would check calibration and subgroup performance by route, airport, and month.
